Quants: Memorize squares up to 30, cubes up to 20, and prime numbers up to 100 to speed up calculations.
Quants: Learn fraction to percentage conversions (e.g., 1/6 = 16.66%, 1/8 = 12.5%) for quick arithmetic.
Quants: In Data Interpretation (DI), approximate values before doing exact calculations to save time.
Quants: Use the unit digit method to quickly eliminate incorrect options in complex multiplications.
Quants: The digital sum (digit root) method can verify calculations without solving the whole equation.
Quants: In time and work problems, using the LCM method (total work = LCM of days) is faster than fractions.
Quants: For simple interest, SI = (P×R×T)/100. For compound interest, remember the effective percentage change formula.
Quants: In Profit and Loss, assume the Cost Price (CP) as 100% to make calculations easier.
Quants: Allegation and mixture formulas can be applied to profit/loss, simple interest, and average speed problems.
Quants: Memorize Pythagorean triplets (3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17) for geometry and mensuration.
Quants: In algebra, substituting values like x=0 or x=1 can often help you arrive at the answer instantly.
Quants: If x + 1/x = a, then x² + 1/x² = a² - 2. Memorize these standard algebraic identities.
Quants: For geometry, familiarize yourself with properties of circles, tangents, and similar triangles (especially for SSC CGL).
Quants: In trigonometry, substituting standard angles (30°, 45°, 60°) can quickly eliminate wrong options.
Quants: The maximum value of a*sin(θ) + b*cos(θ) is √(a² + b²) and the minimum is -√(a² + b²).
Quants: Memorize the volume and surface area formulas for all 3D shapes: cylinder, cone, sphere, hemisphere.
Quants: For average speed, if distance is constant, use 2xy/(x+y).
Quants: Relative speed: Add speeds if moving in opposite directions, subtract if moving in the same direction.
Quants: Trains crossing a platform: Total distance = Length of train + Length of platform.
Quants: In number series, check for constant differences, alternate series, squares, cubes, and prime numbers.
Reasoning: In syllogism, always draw a Venn diagram. Do not assume anything outside the given statements.
Reasoning: For blood relations, draw a family tree. Use squares for males, circles for females, and connect generations vertically.
Reasoning: In coding-decoding, write down the alphabet and their numerical values (A=1, B=2, Z=26) on your rough sheet before the exam starts.
Reasoning: Remember the opposite pairs in the alphabet: A-Z, B-Y, C-X, D-W. They appear frequently in coding questions.
Reasoning: For seating arrangements, always start with definite information. Avoid assuming left/right until the direction they face is clear.
Reasoning: In circular seating arrangements, if people face inward, left is clockwise; if they face outward, left is anti-clockwise.
Reasoning: In puzzles, read all conditions once before placing variables. Connect related conditions together.
Reasoning: For inequalities, understand the 'gate' concept (open gate/closed gate) to quickly determine if a conclusion follows.
Reasoning: In direction sense, remember Pythagoras' theorem is often needed to find the shortest distance.
Reasoning: To quickly solve counting figures (like triangles), learn the standard formulas and tricks for symmetric figures.
Reasoning: For calendar problems, memorize the odd days concept: ordinary year has 1 odd day, leap year has 2 odd days.
Reasoning: In clock problems, the angle between hands is |30*H - 5.5*M|.
Reasoning: In alphanumeric series, pay attention to the exact words 'preceded by' (comes before) and 'followed by' (comes after).
Reasoning: For machine input-output, identify the pattern in the final step first, then check how the elements are shifting per step.
Reasoning: In data sufficiency, you do not need to find the final answer. Just check if the data is enough to find it.
Reasoning: Don't spend more than 2-3 minutes on a single puzzle. If it doesn't click, move on and return later.
English: Read a reputed newspaper daily (like The Hindu or Indian Express) to naturally improve vocabulary and comprehension.
English: When solving reading comprehension (RC), read the questions first before diving into the passage to locate answers faster.
English: In RC, avoid extremes. Options with words like 'always', 'never', 'only' are usually incorrect.
English: For cloze test, read the whole paragraph once without looking at options to understand the tone and context.
English: In error spotting, always check Subject-Verb Agreement first. It is the most tested grammar rule.
English: Singular subjects take singular verbs (e.g., 'Each of the boys IS'). Plural subjects take plural verbs.
English: Understand the difference between 'few' (hardly any, negative) and 'a few' (some, positive).
English: 'Scarcely' and 'Hardly' are followed by 'when', whereas 'No sooner' is followed by 'than'.
English: Prepositions are crucial. Memorize fixed prepositions (e.g., 'fond of', 'accustomed to', 'abstain from').
English: In active/passive voice, the tense NEVER changes. Only the structure changes to Object + Be + V3 + by + Subject.
English: In direct/indirect speech, if the reporting verb is in past tense, the tense inside the quotes must change to the corresponding past tense.
English: For para jumbles, look for opening sentences (independent thoughts) and mandatory pairs (pronoun references, transition words).
English: Understand conditional sentences: Type 1 (If present, will future), Type 2 (If past, would + V1), Type 3 (If past perfect, would have + V3).
English: Learn the nuances of vocabulary by understanding root words, prefixes, and suffixes.
English: 'Since' is used for a point in time (since 1995), while 'for' is used for a duration (for 10 years).
English: Maintain a vocabulary notebook. Jot down new words, their meanings, synonyms, and antonyms.
General Awareness: For SSC, focus heavily on static GK: History, Geography, Polity, Science, and Economics.
General Awareness: Memorize important Articles of the Indian Constitution, especially Fundamental Rights (12-35) and DPSP (36-51).
General Awareness: In History, focus on the Indus Valley Civilization, Buddhism/Jainism, Mauryan/Gupta empires, Delhi Sultanate, Mughals, and the Indian National Movement.
General Awareness: For Geography, know the river systems of India, their origins, tributaries, and major dams.
General Awareness: Learn the major national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, and biosphere reserves in India.
General Awareness: In Science, Biology carries the most weight in SSC. Focus on human diseases, vitamins, and the classification of organisms.
General Awareness: Chemistry: Memorize common names and chemical formulas (e.g., Baking soda = NaHCO3, Washing soda = Na2CO3).
General Awareness: Physics: Know the SI units of important physical quantities and basic concepts of light and sound.
General Awareness: Stay updated with the last 6 months of current affairs before your exam date.
General Awareness: Focus on recent sports events (Olympics, World Cups, IPL), winners, and venues.
General Awareness: Memorize the heads of important national and international organizations (UN, WHO, RBI, SEBI, NITI Aayog).
General Awareness: Keep track of recent index rankings of India (e.g., Happiness Index, Press Freedom Index).
General Awareness: Know the latest Nobel Prize winners and major national awards (Padma Awards, Khel Ratna).
Banking Awareness: For IBPS, understand the structure and functions of the Reserve Bank of India (RBI).
Banking Awareness: Know the monetary policy rates: Repo Rate, Reverse Repo Rate, CRR, SLR, MSF, and Bank Rate.
Banking Awareness: Understand the concept of Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) and the SARFAESI Act.
Banking Awareness: Learn the different types of bank accounts (CASA, RAHA) and their features.
Banking Awareness: Familiarize yourself with digital payment systems: NEFT, RTGS, IMPS, UPI, and their limits.
Banking Awareness: Know the headquarters of all public sector banks and their taglines.
Banking Awareness: Understand Priority Sector Lending (PSL) and its targets for different types of banks.
Banking Awareness: Learn about Basel Norms and Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR).
Banking Awareness: Differentiate between Money Market (short term, regulated by RBI) and Capital Market (long term, regulated by SEBI).
Banking Awareness: Know about negotiable instruments like Cheques, Demand Drafts, and Promissory Notes.
Banking Awareness: Understand inflation: types (Demand-pull, Cost-push) and indices (WPI, CPI).
General Tips: Always attempt mock tests in a timed, exam-like environment to build stamina and speed.
General Tips: Analyze your mock tests thoroughly. Identify which questions took the most time and find shortcut methods.
General Tips: Do not let your ego get stuck on a difficult question. Skip it and secure the easy marks first.
General Tips: Accuracy is more important than speed. Negative marking can drastically lower your percentile.
General Tips: During the exam, follow a sequence. Start with your strongest section to boost confidence.
General Tips: For SSC CGL, Tier 1 is a speed test (100 questions in 60 mins). Practice reading questions rapidly.
General Tips: For IBPS PO/Clerk, reasoning and quants are often calculation-heavy. Build a habit of mental math.
General Tips: Maintain a separate notebook for writing down all your mistakes from mock tests and revise it weekly.
General Tips: Take proper rest the night before the exam. A fatigued brain makes silly calculation mistakes.
General Tips: Familiarize yourself with the exam interface (scrolling, mark for review, save and next).
General Tips: Use the 'Mark for Review' feature wisely for questions you know how to solve but are lengthy.
General Tips: Read the instructions carefully. Sometimes they ask for the 'incorrect' statement rather than the 'correct' one.
General Tips: In Data Interpretation, always read the units carefully (thousands, millions, percentages).
General Tips: If you are guessing, try to eliminate at least two options first. Do not blind guess.
General Tips: Keep your rough sheet organized during the exam so you don't lose track of intermediate calculations.
General Tips: SSC repeats previous year questions heavily. Solve at least the last 3-4 years of PYQs.
General Tips: IBPS rarely repeats exact questions, but the pattern of puzzles and DI remains similar. Focus on concepts.
General Tips: Consistency is key. Studying 4 hours every day is better than studying 12 hours once a week.
General Tips: Stay calm. If a section is unusually tough, it is tough for everyone. The cutoff will drop.
General Tips: Revise vocabulary and current affairs in short 15-minute bursts multiple times a day.
General Tips: Use flashcards for formulas, important dates, and static GK facts.
General Tips: For descriptive papers, practice writing essays and letters on current trending topics.
General Tips: Read the editorial section of a newspaper daily to build opinions and arguments for descriptive exams and interviews.
General Tips: Don't ignore typing tests in SSC. Practice typing daily for 15-20 minutes to maintain speed and accuracy.
